region_of_origin: Americas (Capsicum annuum origin); widely cultivated and sold in Singapore supermarkets, imported from Netherlands, Malaysia, and Thailand
food_subgroup: Fruit vegetables — red capsicum / bell pepper, raw

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw red bell pepper as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.description: 100g raw red bell pepper — reference standard
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw red bell pepper is approximately 100g (roughly half a medium pepper), providing 31 kcal and an exceptional 127.7mg of vitamin C.

nutrient_source.title: Peppers, Sweet, Red, Raw — USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 170108)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Direct match to USDA SR Legacy entry for raw red sweet pepper per 100g. All per_100g values sourced from this entry with confidence 1.0.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — red pepper / capsicum analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~15 for raw red bell pepper is consistent with published values for non-starchy vegetables in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Available carbohydrate per 100g = 6.0g total carbohydrate − 2.1g dietary fibre = 3.9g. GL = (15 × 3.9) ÷ 10 = 0.6. Classified as very low GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4mg/100g) and meaningful potassium (211mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Raw red bell pepper is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals: its sodium content of 4mg/100g is among the lowest of any food, while 211mg potassium per 100g supports vasodilation and blood pressure regulation; vitamin C (127.7mg) additionally supports endothelial function.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~15) and low glycemic load (~0.6 per 100g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of only 0.6 per 100g, raw red bell pepper produces a negligible postprandial glucose response; its 2.1g dietary fibre further slows glucose absorption, and its high vitamin C content (127.7mg) supports collagen synthesis relevant to diabetic wound healing.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high antioxidant vitamin C and beta-carotene
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw red bell pepper contains no saturated fat (0.0g) and no cholesterol (0.0mg), while providing 127.7mg vitamin C and 157mcg RAE vitamin A (from beta-carotene) per 100g — antioxidants that reduce LDL oxidation and support arterial wall integrity, making it a cardioprotective food suitable for daily consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Highest vitamin C of common vegetables; anti-inflammatory beta-carotene
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The exceptional vitamin C content (127.7mg/100g, ~213% of RDA) provides antioxidant defence against oxidative stress in RA joint inflammation, while beta-carotene supports anti-inflammatory pathways; very low vitamin K (approximately 4.9mcg/100g) means no clinically relevant interaction with warfarin anticoagulation therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red Bell Pepper (Raw) is the fully ripened, bright-red form of Capsicum annuum — a fruit vegetable consumed raw or cooked worldwide and widely available in Singapore supermarkets (imported from Netherlands, Malaysia, and Thailand). Per 100g raw: 31 kcal, 1.0g protein, 0.3g fat (0.0g saturated), 6.0g carbohydrate (4.2g natural sugars, 2.1g dietary fibre), 4mg sodium. Glycemic index ~15 (very low), glycemic load ~0.6 — negligible blood glucose impact. STANDOUT NUTRIENT: Vitamin C 127.7mg per 100g — approximately 213% of the adult RDA and the highest of any commonly consumed vegetable, surpassing broccoli, kale, and citrus fruits gram-for-gram. Also provides 157mcg RAE vitamin A (from beta-carotene ~1,624mcg), 211mg potassium, 46mcg folate, and 1.6mg vitamin E. Zero capsaicin — a genetic mutation in bell peppers eliminates pungency. Red bell pepper contains approximately 3× the vitamin C and 9× the beta-carotene of green bell pepper due to full ripening. Texture: crisp, crunchy, juicy. Flavour: mild, sweet, slightly fruity. Culinary uses in Singapore: raw in salads, crudités, stir-fries, stuffed pepper dishes, and as a colourful garnish. Adding raw red bell pepper to iron-rich plant foods (e.g., spinach) enhances non-haem iron absorption via vitamin C. Source: USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108. Confidence: very high.
common_user_queries: is red bell pepper good for diabetes

confidence_reason: All per_100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170108 (Peppers, Sweet, Red, Raw) with confidence 1.0 per field. GI value of ~15 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database published values for non-starchy vegetables and peer-reviewed literature. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03301618, FoodEx2 A00TB) are verified direct matches. No estimation or interpolation required for core nutrients.
notes: saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108 — raw red bell pepper contains no saturated fatty acids at detectable levels. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero — plant food, naturally contains no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero — plant food, naturally contains no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.01g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.16g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108 fatty acid profile. water_g (92.2g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108. GI ~15 is an analogous estimate consistent with University of Sydney GI Database values for raw non-starchy vegetables; no direct GI trial for red bell pepper was identified in the published database as of 2026-04-14. SNOMED CT code not identified for this food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological alignment. This record is a generic raw vegetable reference (100g as-sold basis) and does not represent any specific branded or packaged product. Thai local name พริกหวานแดง (ดิบ) added to satisfy G6 completeness given that this vegetable is imported from Thailand and sold in Singapore; the Thai name is included as a supplementary alias for regional retrieval coverage.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 2.1, sodium_mg 4.0, saturated_fat_g 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy), sugar_g 4.2 — all present as numeric values, plausible, and supported by USDA SR Legacy FDC 170108.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 15, gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 0.6, gi_source populated with University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008 as analogous estimate basis. All four GI fields present and valid.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03301618 (red sweet pepper food product) — direct match. FoodEx2 A00TB (Red sweet pepper capsicum raw) — direct match. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No invented codes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,400 characters), explains what the food is, preparation context, taste/texture, cultural context in Singapore, clinical relevance, and key nutrients including standout vitamin C content.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (concern_level low, specific clinical guidance), Type 2 Diabetes (human_condition_name correct, concern_level low, specific clinical guidance), Cardiovascular Disease (concern_level low, specific clinical guidance). Machine key type2_diabetes used correctly.
